courseName: Network Operations: Operate, Validate and Secure Automated Networks

modules.title: Telemetry and Observability
modules.subtitle: Streaming Network State with Model-Driven Telemetry

slides.name: snapshots-arent-enough
slides.script: Courses one and two taught you to automate VLANs, store intent in Git, run CI/CD pipelines, simulate in containers, and treat a source of truth as golden. Course three asks a different question: once the right config is deployed, how do you know the network is healthy right now — not five minutes ago when someone ran show commands? Polling devices on a timer misses spikes, brownouts, and silent drift. Model-driven telemetry streams structured metrics and state changes continuously from the device to collectors your automation stack can read. Exam objective three point one lives here. You do not need to be a Grafana expert on day one — you need to understand the architecture and run one lab stream end to end.
slides.title: Why Snapshots Are Not Enough

slides.name: polling-vs-streaming
slides.script: Traditional monitoring often polls: every five minutes an NMS sends SNMP get requests and stores whatever counters happened to be at that instant. That works for slow-moving links but misses a thirty-second CPU spike or a flap that cleared before the next poll. Streaming telemetry pushes updates on a schedule you define — every second, or on change — over a persistent gRPC session. The device is the publisher; your collector listens. For automation, streaming data feeds validation scripts, dashboards, and anomaly detectors without pretending the network stands still between polls.
slides.title: Polling vs Streaming Telemetry
points: Traditional monitoring often polls every five minutes an NMS
SNMP get requests and stores whatever counters happened
That works for slow — moving links but misses

slides.name: what-model-driven-telemetry-means
slides.script: Model-driven telemetry, often shortened to MDT, means the data leaving the device follows YANG models the platform already understands — not random text you parse by hand. A path like interfaces-state slash interface slash statistics tells you exactly which counters exist and their types. The model is the contract between producer and consumer. When your collector requests or receives that path, both sides agree on field names and units. That is why exam three point one focuses on architectural components — producer, transport, subscription, and consumer — rather than memorizing every OID from legacy SNMP.
slides.title: Model-Driven Telemetry in Plain Language
points: Model — driven telemetry often shortened to MDT
YANG models the platform already understands — not random
The model is the contract between

slides.name: snmp-poll-vs-gnmi-subscribe
slides.script: SNMP polling asks the device for values on a schedule the NMS controls. The device answers and forgets until the next poll. gNMI subscribe — part of the gRPC Network Management Interface — lets a collector open a subscription: give me this YANG path every N milliseconds, or when it changes. The session stays open; updates flow as events. SNMP remains on many networks for legacy gear. Modern IOS XE, NX-OS, and automation labs lean on gRPC and gNMI for structured streaming. The exam expects you to describe both patterns and when streaming wins.
slides.title: SNMP Poll vs gNMI Subscribe

slides.name: mdt-architecture-components
slides.script: Exam three point one names four architectural pieces you should sketch from memory. The data producer is the network device — router or switch — running a telemetry agent that reads modeled data. The transport is usually gRPC over TCP, sometimes with TLS. A subscription ties a YANG path or sensor group to an update policy — periodic or on change — and a destination address. The consumer is anything that ingests the stream: Prometheus with a gNMI exporter, Telegraf, Kafka, or your own Python client. Missing any piece means silence on the collector side even when config looks correct.
slides.title: Four MDT Architecture Components
points: Exam three point one names
Four architectural pieces you should sketch from memory
The data producer is the network device — router

slides.name: yang-models-in-telemetry
slides.script: YANG models from module four of course two reappear here as the vocabulary of telemetry. Cisco publishes models such as Cisco-IOS-XE-interfaces-oper for interface counters and Cisco-IOS-XE-platform-oper for CPU and memory. A subscription references these paths — either native XPath or OpenConfig equivalents depending on platform. The collector does not invent field names; it decodes what the model defines. When troubleshooting empty streams, verify the path exists on your software version with show platform software yang-management process. Wrong model or wrong path is the most common lab mistake after a typo in the destination IP.
slides.title: YANG Models Define What You Can Stream
points: YANG models from module four of course two reappear
Cisco — IOS — XE — platform — oper

slides.name: dial-out-vs-dial-in
slides.script: Two deployment patterns appear on exams and in production. Dial-out — sometimes called sensor-driven or push — means the device initiates the gRPC connection to the collector when a subscription is active. Dial-in means the collector connects to the device and establishes subscriptions remotely. IOS XE model-driven telemetry commonly uses dial-out to a receiver IP and port you configure under telemetry ietf subscription. gNMI clients often dial in to port five seven four zero zero on the device. Firewalls and TLS policies differ between the two; know which direction opens the session in your design.
slides.title: Dial-Out vs Dial-In Telemetry
leftItems: Common in IOS XE MDT subscriptions
rightItems: Firewall must allow inbound to device

slides.name: lab-setup-telemetry
slides.script: Welcome to the telemetry lab. Create a folder named netops_ops_lab beside your course two netops_iac_lab — course three focuses on operate and observe, not replace your Git repo. Inside netops_ops_lab add a docker-compose dot yaml that runs Prometheus and a gNMI or MDT collector image; the exact file is on the next slides. You need reachability from a lab router or CML node to the collector IP on port five seven five zero zero for gRPC. Confirm docker compose version succeeds. This lab never touches production; use sandbox credentials only.
slides.title: Start the Telemetry Lab
points: Telemetry lab
Create a folder named netops_ops_lab beside your course
Operate and observe not replace your

slides.name: docker-compose-telemetry-stack
slides.script: Line one creates the project folder. Line two moves into it. Lines four through twenty define docker-compose dot yaml with Prometheus on port nine zero nine zero and a collector service that listens for gRPC dial-out on five seven five zero zero. Line twenty-two starts the stack in detached mode. After this slide Prometheus UI is available at localhost colon nine zero nine zero and the collector waits for the device to connect. Adjust image names to match your lab registry if needed.
slides.title: Start Collector and Prometheus
slides.code:
mkdir netops_ops_lab && cd netops_ops_lab
cat > docker-compose.yml << 'EOF'
services:
  prometheus:
    image: prom/prometheus:latest
    ports:
      - "9090:9090"
    volumes:
      - ./prometheus.yml:/etc/prometheus/prometheus.yml
  gnmic-collector:
    image: ghcr.io/openconfig/gnmic:latest
    command: subscribe --config /cfg/gnmic.yaml
    ports:
      - "57500:57500"
    volumes:
      - ./gnmic.yaml:/cfg/gnmic.yaml
EOF
docker compose up -d

slides.name: iosxe-telemetry-subscription
slides.script: These lines belong on a lab IOS XE device — console or automation session — not on your laptop. Line one enters configuration mode. Lines two through nine define an IETF telemetry subscription numbered one zero one with yang-push encoding, a filter on the interfaces-state YANG path, periodic updates every one thousand milliseconds, and a gRPC receiver pointing at your collector IP. Line ten commits. Replace ten dot ten dot ten dot five with the docker host address your lab router can reach. After commit, the device dials out to the collector.
slides.title: Configure IOS XE MDT Subscription
slides.code:
configure terminal
telemetry ietf subscription 101
 encoding encode-kvgpb
 filter xpath-native /Cisco-IOS-XE-interfaces-oper:interfaces-state/interface
 stream yang-push
 update-policy periodic 1000
 receiver ip address 10.10.10.5 57500 protocol grpc-tcp
 exit
 exit
commit

slides.name: gnmi-path-and-sample-interval
slides.script: If your lab uses gNMI dial-in instead of dial-out, configure the collector side. Lines one through twelve write gnmic dot yaml subscribing to interface counters every five seconds and forwarding to Prometheus. Line fourteen reloads the collector container. The path uses OpenConfig interfaces model — adjust to Cisco native paths if your device requires them. Sample interval and on-change policies are exam vocabulary; periodic means send every N milliseconds even when values are flat.
slides.title: Collector gNMI Subscription File
slides.code:
cat > gnmic.yaml << 'EOF'
subscriptions:
  iosxe-if-stats:
    paths:
      - path: /interfaces/interface/state/counters
        stream-mode: sample
        sample-interval: 5s
    stream-mode: sample
outputs:
  prom:
    type: prometheus
    listen: :9804
EOF
docker compose restart gnmic-collector

slides.name: collect-interface-counters
slides.script: Line one queries Prometheus for a metric your collector exported — name varies by model; in-in-octets or similar interface counter. Line two uses curl against the Prometheus HTTP API with a query parameter. You should see non-zero time series when traffic crosses the lab link. Line four runs tcpdump briefly on the collector host to confirm gRPC packets on five seven five zero zero if metrics are empty — transport problems show up before application problems.
slides.title: Query Interface Counters
slides.code:
curl -s 'http://localhost:9090/api/v1/query?query=if_in_octets' | jq .
# optional: confirm gRPC on collector
sudo tcpdump -ni any port 57500 -c 10

slides.name: export-telemetry-prometheus
slides.script: Prometheus scrapes the collector exporter, not the router directly. Lines one through eight add a scrape job pointing at gnmic-collector port nine eight zero four on the docker network. Line ten reloads Prometheus configuration with curl post to the reload endpoint — or restart the container if reload is disabled. Line twelve opens the Prometheus targets page conceptually — all targets should show UP before you declare the lab success.
slides.title: Wire Prometheus to the Collector
slides.code:
cat > prometheus.yml << 'EOF'
global:
  scrape_interval: 15s
scrape_configs:
  - job_name: gnmic
    static_configs:
      - targets: ['gnmic-collector:9804']
EOF
curl -X POST http://localhost:9090/-/reload

slides.name: verify-streaming-metrics
slides.script: Verification belongs on the device and the collector. Line one shows active telemetry subscriptions on IOS XE. Line two shows the gRPC connection state — connected means dial-out succeeded. Line three lists Prometheus series count — increasing over time proves streaming, not a one-shot poll. If connected is false, check routing, ACLs, and destination IP. If connected is true but Prometheus is empty, check YANG path and encoding mismatch.
slides.title: Verify End-to-End Streaming
slides.code:
show telemetry ietf subscription 101 detail
show telemetry connection
curl -s 'http://localhost:9090/api/v1/series?match[]={__name__=~"if.*"}' | jq '.data | length'

slides.name: troubleshoot-no-telemetry-data
slides.script: When the stream is silent, work the architecture stack top to bottom. Subscription not committed or wrong path — device sends nothing. ACL or NAT blocks gRPC — connection never shows connected. Encoding mismatch — collector receives garbage it drops. Prometheus scrape misconfigured — metrics exist on collector but not in PromQL. The exam may give log snippets; map each symptom to one layer.
slides.title: Troubleshoot Empty Telemetry
points: Check subscription and YANG path on device
Confirm gRPC connected and port open
Validate collector output and Prom scrape
slides.code:
show telemetry internal subscription
show platform software yang-management process
ping 10.10.10.5 source <router-loopback>

modules.title: Logging and Diagnostics
modules.subtitle: Syslog, Webhooks, and Root Cause Analysis

slides.name: telemetry-to-logging-bridge
slides.script: Module one gave you a telemetry stream — interface counters flowing into Prometheus through gRPC. That answers how busy the network is. It does not answer why last night's Ansible job failed on switch three, or who triggered a rollback at two AM. Metrics without logs leave you guessing. Module two implements a logging strategy for network automation — exam objective three point two — and teaches you to diagnose failures from log output related to an event — objective three point three. Syslog captures device and platform events. Webhooks push pipeline and application events to your automation stack. Together they form the evidence trail validation and security modules build on.
slides.title: From Metrics to Event Logs

slides.name: metrics-without-logs-blind-spots
slides.script: Prometheus may show interface errors climbing while your playbook log sits on one engineer's laptop. GitLab shows a red pipeline X but not the Ansible traceback unless you configured job artifacts and logging. A syslog gap means you cannot correlate a BGP flap timestamp with a config push. Logging strategy means deciding what to record, at which severity, where to send it, and how long to retain it — before an incident, not during one. Network automation multiplies actors: devices, runners, playbooks, APIs, and webhooks. Each layer must emit events to shared sinks your team can search.
slides.title: What Metrics Alone Cannot Tell You
points: Prometheus may show interface errors climbing while your
Playbook log sits on one engineer's laptop
GitLab shows a red pipeline X but not

slides.name: what-logging-strategy-means
slides.script: Exam three point two asks you to implement a logging strategy targeting destinations such as syslog or webhooks. In plain language: choose log sources — Python scripts, Ansible, GitLab jobs, IOS XE — pick a format — structured JSON beats unparsed text for search — select transports — UDP or TCP syslog, HTTPS webhook POST — and define retention and access control. A strategy document lists severity levels, required fields like timestamp and request ID, and which destination receives which event class. Device config changes might go to syslog. Pipeline failures might hit a webhook that opens a ticket. One size does not fit all destinations.
slides.title: Logging Strategy in Plain Language
points: Exam three point two asks you to implement
In plain language choose log sources
Python scripts Ansible

slides.name: syslog-vs-application-logs
slides.script: Syslog is the decades-old standard network devices speak — RFC five four two four style messages with facility, severity, and hostname. Routers send link up, BGP neighbor down, and login events to a syslog server. Application logs come from your Python validator, Ansible callback plugins, or GitLab runner shell — often JSON with custom keys like playbook name and inventory host. Both belong in a network automation logging strategy. Syslog covers the underlay and control plane. Application logs cover intent, pipeline stages, and API calls. The exam expects you to wire both destination types, not pick one.
slides.title: Syslog vs Application Logs
leftItems: UDP or TCP to syslog server

slides.name: logging-architecture-components
slides.script: Mirror the telemetry mental model with four logging pieces. The event producer emits a record — a switch syslog line or a Python log dot info call. The format encodes fields — plain syslog text or JSON. The transport delivers — syslog forwarder, HTTPS webhook, or file shipper. The sink stores and indexes — rsyslog files, Elasticsearch, Splunk, or a webhook handler database. A broken strategy usually omits correlation IDs — you cannot tie a pipeline job to a device config change without a shared request or change ticket ID in both log streams.
slides.title: Four Logging Architecture Pieces
points: Mirror the telemetry mental model with four logging pieces
Python log dot
The format encodes fields — plain syslog text

slides.name: structured-vs-unstructured-logs
slides.script: Unstructured logs read well to humans — Ansible failed on host sw1 — but grep and SIEM queries struggle. Structured logs use key value or JSON fields: timestamp, level, request_id, host, playbook, task, error. Automation teams standardize on JSON lines — one JSON object per line — so jq and log aggregators filter fast. Exam scenarios may show a log snippet and ask what field proves which device failed; structured fields make that trivial. Python logging dot basicConfig with a JSON formatter is enough for lab work; production adds schema versioning.
slides.title: Structured Logs for Automation
points: Unstructured logs read well
Ansible failed on host sw1 — but grep
JSON fields timestamp level request_id host playbook task error

slides.name: event-workflows-for-automation
slides.script: Event workflow means what happens after something is logged — not just storing text. A typical automation flow: trigger — merge to main starts pipeline — emit — each stage logs start and end with job ID — correlate — same change ID in GitLab, Ansible, and syslog — alert — webhook fires on ERROR level — act — engineer or runbook responds. Module one telemetry might trigger an alert on threshold; module two logging explains why the threshold moved. Design workflows so noisy INFO logs do not page on-call while CRITICAL webhook failures do.
slides.title: Event Workflows
points: Event workflow means what happens after something is logged
And end with job ID —
ID in GitLab Ansible

slides.name: root-cause-analysis-plain-language
slides.script: Root cause analysis, RCA, is not blame — it is reconstructing a timeline from evidence. Exam three point three gives you logs and output related to an event and asks what failed and why. Start at the symptom — pipeline red, VLAN missing — walk backward through timestamps — prevalidation passed at ten oh two, deploy failed at ten oh four — compare layers — was intent wrong, inventory wrong, or device unreachable? Hypothesis, test with one log query, confirm or eliminate. The wrong answer picks the first error line without reading context above it.
slides.title: Root Cause Analysis Steps
points: Root cause analysis RCA is not blame —
Event and asks what failed and why Start
VLAN missing — walk backward through timestamps — prevalidation

slides.name: diagnose-automation-from-logs
slides.script: Three failure patterns repeat on the AUTOCOR exam for three point three. Missing dependency — job log shows ModuleNotFoundError or command not found — fix requirements or image. Wrong inventory — Ansible skips hosts or targets production — log shows UNREACHABLE or wrong IP. Assertion or validation failure — post-validation script exits one — log shows assert VLAN two hundred missing though deploy reported ok. Webhook not received — pipeline succeeded locally but SIEM empty — check URL, TLS, and firewall. Always read the first ERROR in context, not the last line alone.

points: Three failure patterns repeat

slides.name: python-structured-logging
slides.script: Lines one through twenty create run_deploy dot py with JSON logging. Each log line includes timestamp, level, request_id from environment variable REQUEST_ID, and message. Line twenty-two sets REQUEST_ID and runs the script — success path logs INFO. Line twenty-three sets SIMULATE_FAILURE equals one to trigger ERROR and exit code one — simulates a failed deploy for RCA practice. This pattern satisfies application-side logging for exam three point two.
slides.title: JSON Logging in Python
slides.code:
mkdir -p scripts webhook
cat > scripts/run_deploy.py << 'EOF'
import json, logging, os, sys, datetime
class JsonFormatter(logging.Formatter):
    def format(self, record):
        return json.dumps({
            "ts": datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat() + "Z",
            "level": record.levelname,
            "request_id": os.environ.get("REQUEST_ID", "none"),
            "msg": record.getMessage(),
        })
log = logging.getLogger("deploy")
log.handlers = [logging.StreamHandler()]
log.handlers[0].setFormatter(JsonFormatter())
log.setLevel(logging.INFO)
if os.environ.get("SIMULATE_FAILURE"):
    log.error("deploy failed: vlan apply timeout on sw1")
    sys.exit(1)
log.info("deploy ok: vlans applied from intent")
EOF
REQUEST_ID=chg-4421 python scripts/run_deploy.py
SIMULATE_FAILURE=1 REQUEST_ID=chg-4421 python scripts/run_deploy.py || true

slides.name: configure-syslog-destination
slides.script: Lines one through twelve add an rsyslog service to docker-compose dot yaml listening on UDP five one four and writing to a host-mounted logs folder. Line fourteen starts the stack. Lines sixteen through eighteen send a test logger message into the container — you should see a line in logs slash syslog dot log. On a lab IOS XE device you would configure logging host pointing at this collector IP — exam three point two syslog destination. Replace ten dot ten dot ten dot ten with your docker host address reachable from the lab router.
slides.title: Syslog Collector in Docker
slides.code:
cat >> docker-compose.yml << 'EOF'
  rsyslog:
    image: rsyslog/syslog_appliance_alpine:latest
    ports:
      - "514:514/udp"
    volumes:
      - ./logs:/var/log
EOF
docker compose up -d rsyslog
logger -n 127.0.0.1 -P 514 -t netops-lab "test syslog from automation host"
tail -3 logs/messages 2>/dev/null || tail -3 logs/syslog

slides.name: webhook-receiver-for-pipeline
slides.script: Lines one through eighteen create a minimal webhook handler. It accepts POST JSON, logs the payload with timestamp and object_kind field from GitLab webhooks, and returns two hundred. Line twenty starts the server on port eight zero eight zero. In GitLab project settings add a webhook URL http colon slash slash your-host colon eight zero eight zero slash hook for pipeline events — lab use HTTP only. Exam three point two explicitly mentions webhooks as a logging destination for automation solutions.
slides.title: Webhook Handler for Pipeline Events
slides.code:
cat > webhook/handler.py << 'EOF'
from http.server import HTTPServer, BaseHTTPRequestHandler
import json, datetime
class Hook(BaseHTTPRequestHandler):
    def do_POST(self):
        n = int(self.headers.get("Content-Length", 0))
        body = json.loads(self.rfile.read(n) or b"{}")
        rec = {"ts": datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat()+"Z",
               "kind": body.get("object_kind"), "status": body.get("build_status")}
        print(json.dumps(rec), flush=True)
        self.send_response(200); self.end_headers()
HTTPServer(("0.0.0.0", 8080), Hook).serve_forever()
EOF
python webhook/handler.py

slides.name: emit-correlated-log-events
slides.script: Correlation requires the same request_id everywhere. Line one exports REQUEST_ID matching a change ticket. Line two runs the deploy script and pipes JSON logs to a file. Line three sends a syslog line embedding that request_id in the message text so device and app logs join in SIEM search. Line four curls the webhook handler with a fake pipeline failure payload including the same request_id in custom fields. Practice searching logs slash deploy dot json and syslog for chg-4421.
slides.title: Correlate Logs With request_id
slides.code:
export REQUEST_ID=chg-4421
SIMULATE_FAILURE=1 python scripts/run_deploy.py 2>&1 | tee logs/deploy.json
logger -n 127.0.0.1 -P 514 -t netops "request_id=chg-4421 interface Gi1/0/1 down"
curl -s -X POST http://127.0.0.1:8080/hook \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"object_kind":"pipeline","build_status":"failed","request_id":"chg-4421"}'

slides.name: trace-failure-by-request-id
slides.script: Exam three point three — given these outputs, what failed? Line one greps deploy JSON for ERROR lines. Line two filters syslog for the same request_id. Line three uses jq to print level and msg fields only — readable timeline. From the evidence: deploy failed on VLAN apply timeout while interface down syslog suggests link problem — root cause is layer one on sw1, not bad YAML. Document your timeline before reading the answer key in your head.
slides.title: RCA Lab — Trace One Failure
slides.code:
grep chg-4421 logs/deploy.json
grep chg-4421 logs/messages 2>/dev/null || grep chg-4421 logs/syslog
grep chg-4421 logs/deploy.json | jq -c '{level,msg}'

slides.name: troubleshoot-webhook-and-syslog
slides.script: When logs never arrive, check transport before blaming the script. Syslog silent — UDP blocked, wrong collector IP, device vrf not reaching server. Webhook silent — GitLab cannot reach your laptop, HTTP blocked, handler not running. JSON empty — script exits before log call. Exam trick: show successful deploy log but failed validation — root cause is post-validation assertion not deploy. Map symptom to layer: producer, transport, sink.

slides.name: logging-to-validation-bridge
slides.script: Module two left you with logs and webhooks that explain why a job failed — or claimed success. Success in a deploy log is not proof the network matches intent. A playbook can return zero without applying every VLAN. Post-validation in course two checked a YAML file on disk, not live device state. Module three implements change validation with pyATS CLI tools — exam objective three point four — and ties validation failure to automated rollback. pyATS is Cisco's automation test framework: testbed files describe devices, jobs run test cases, and learn plus diff capture before-and-after state. You will run validation in netops_ops_lab after a simulated deploy, then roll back when assertions fail.
slides.title: Logs Are Not Validation

slides.name: deploy-success-without-proof
slides.script: Three false confidence traps appear in network automation incidents. Ansible recap shows ok equals three changed equals zero — but check mode was never off on the right host. GitLab post-validation passed — but the Python script only parsed intent files, not running config. Syslog shows config commit — but the wrong feature was edited. Change validation means testing network state against expected outcomes after the change window — not trusting green UI alone. pyATS adds device-side test cases and structured learn snapshots the exam expects you to invoke from the CLI.
slides.title: When Deploy Looks Successful
points: Three false confidence traps appear in network automation incidents
Ansible recap shows ok equals three changed equals zero
GitLab post — validation passed —

slides.name: what-change-validation-means
slides.script: Exam three point four — implement change validation using pyATS CLI tools. Change validation is the gate that asks: after automation ran, does the network actually match what we intended? It includes pre-change baseline capture, post-change test execution, and a documented pass or fail. pyATS supplies the CLI entry points — pyats run job, pyats learn, pyats diff — and Python test cases under the aetest framework. Validation runs in CI/CD post-validation stage or as a manual gate before you close a change ticket. Fail validation and you do not celebrate — you rollback or fix forward with evidence.
slides.title: Change Validation in Plain Language
points: Exam three point four — implement change validation
CLI tools Change validation
Gate that asks after automation ran does

slides.name: testing-strategy-for-automation
slides.script: A testing strategy layers gates before and after deploy. Pre-change — lint intent, dry-run playbook, capture pyATS learn snapshot as baseline. Change window — apply automation with request_id in logs from module two. Post-change — pyats run job with test cases for VLANs, routing neighbors, or API responses. Regression — optional nightly jobs on lab testbed. The exam cares that you can place pyATS in the post-change slot and interpret job output — passed, failed, errored — not that you built a full enterprise test pyramid.
slides.title: Testing Strategy Layers
points: A testing strategy layers gates before and after deploy
Pre — change — lint intent dry — run
Change window — apply

slides.name: pyats-plain-language
slides.script: pyATS — Python Automated Test System — is Cisco's framework for network test automation. A testbed YAML file lists devices, addresses, credentials, and OS types. A job file orchestrates sections — common setup connects devices, test cases assert state, common cleanup disconnects. Easypy wraps jobs for reporting. The CLI you need for AUTOCOR: pyats version shows install; pyats run job executes a job against a testbed; pyats learn captures device state to files; pyats diff compares snapshots. Install with pip install pyats pyats dot contrib in a virtualenv on your runner — same machine as Ansible is fine.
slides.title: pyATS in Plain Language
points: Python Automated Test System — is Cisco's framework
A testbed YAML file lists devices addresses credentials
Job file orchestrates sections — common setup connects devices

slides.name: pyats-architecture-components
slides.script: Four pyATS pieces mirror telemetry and logging architecture. Testbed — inventory of devices under test — like Ansible inventory but pyATS native. Job — Python file with aetest sections and test cases. Runtime — pyats run job invokes Easypy to execute and log results. Artifacts — learn snapshots, diff output, and job reports stored on disk for audit. In GitLab post-validation stage you call pyats run job and fail the pipeline if exit code is non-zero. Keep testbed credentials in CI variables — module four covers secret management properly.
slides.title: Four pyATS Components
points: Four pyATS pieces mirror telemetry and logging
Testbed — inventory of devices
Under test

slides.name: pre-change-vs-post-change-validation
slides.script: Pre-change validation prevents bad intent from touching devices — yamllint, ansible minus minus check, pyats learn before the window opens. Post-change validation proves the live network — pyats run job asserting VLAN one hundred exists, BGP neighbor is Established, or HTTP API returns two hundred. The exam may ask where pyATS fits — answer post-change device validation unless the question explicitly describes baseline capture. Learn before, run job after, diff if you need to prove only intended deltas landed.
slides.title: Pre-Change vs Post-Change

slides.name: change-assurance-workflow
slides.script: Change assurance is the end-to-end story your capstone will reuse. Step one — baseline with pyats learn or Git tag on intent. Step two — deploy with request_id logged. Step three — pyats run job post-validation. Step four — pass closes the change; fail triggers rollback. Step five — pyats learn again or diff proves rollback restored state. Modules one and two supply telemetry and logs for timeline; module three supplies the pass-fail gate. Document each step in the change ticket — exam scenarios expect you to order them correctly.
slides.title: Change Assurance Workflow
points: Change assurance is the end — to — end
Git tag on intent Step two — deploy
With request_id logged

slides.name: automated-rollback-when-validation-fails
slides.script: Automated rollback means validation failure starts a scripted undo — not a panicked SSH session. Common patterns: git revert the merge that triggered deploy, re-run ansible-playbook with previous intent, or push last-known-good from source of truth. pyATS re-run confirms rollback — second pyats run job should pass. Wire rollback in GitLab as a manual job or a failed-post-validation trigger with strict rules so it never runs on flaky tests. Same request_id from module two ties validation logs to rollback logs. Fail closed — if validation is unreachable, do not mark the change complete.
slides.title: Rollback When Validation Fails
points: Automated rollback means validation failure starts a scripted undo
Ansible — playbook
With previous intent or push last — known —

Use lab device credentials only; never point pyATS at production without approval.

slides.name: lab-setup-pyats
slides.script: Inside netops_ops_lab create pyats slash testbed dot yaml, pyats slash jobs slash validate_vlans dot py, and a requirements-pyats dot txt with pinned pyats and genie versions. Activate the same Python virtualenv you used for Ansible or create venv dot pyats. Your testbed needs at least one lab router or switch reachable by SSH — CML, VIRL, or a sandbox from course one. If you have no live device today, the job includes a localhost mock section so you can practice CLI flow; swap in real hostnames before the exam lab.
slides.title: Extend netops_ops_lab for pyATS
points: Inside netops_ops_lab create pyats slash testbed dot yaml pyats
Requirements — pyats dot txt with pinned pyats

slides.name: install-pyats-toolchain
slides.script: Line one creates a dedicated virtualenv for pyATS to avoid clashing with Ansible deps. Line two activates it. Lines four through six pin pyats, genie, and pyats dot contrib versions in requirements-pyats dot txt. Line eight installs. Line nine confirms CLI — pyats version must print a version string. Genie bundles parsers pyATS uses for show command output on Cisco platforms.
slides.title: Install pyATS and Genie
slides.code:
cd netops_ops_lab
python3 -m venv venv-pyats
source venv-pyats/bin/activate  # Windows: venv-pyats\Scripts\activate
cat > requirements-pyats.txt << 'EOF'
pyats[full]==24.11
pyats.contrib==24.11
genie==24.11
EOF
pip install -r requirements-pyats.txt
pyats version

slides.name: define-testbed-yaml
slides.script: Lines one through sixteen write testbed dot yaml. Device lab-sw1 uses IOS XE with SSH — replace the IP and credentials with your sandbox. The testbed is the inventory pyATS run job reads — exam three point four expects you to know testbed structure, not memorize every optional key. Never commit production passwords; use environment variables or vault in real repos — module four covers secrets.
slides.title: Create pyATS Testbed
slides.code:
mkdir -p pyats/jobs pyats/snapshots
cat > pyats/testbed.yaml << 'EOF'
testbed:
  name: netops_ops_lab
  devices:
    lab-sw1:
      type: switch
      os: iosxe
      credentials:
        default:
          username: admin
          password: "${LAB_SSH_PASSWORD}"
      connections:
        cli:
          protocol: ssh
          ip: 192.168.100.11
EOF

slides.name: pyats-learn-baseline
slides.script: Before the change window, capture baseline state. Line one exports the lab password from your shell — same pattern as module four preview. Line two runs pyats learn on lab-sw1 for the vlan brief feature — output lands under pyats slash snapshots slash pre. Line three lists files created. Learn snapshots are evidence if someone asks what changed during the window. On platforms without learn support in your lab, skip to run job with a parse of show vlan brief inside the test case instead.
slides.title: Baseline With pyats learn
slides.code:
export LAB_SSH_PASSWORD='lab-only'
pyats learn vlan brief --devices lab-sw1 \
  --testbed pyats/testbed.yaml \
  --output pyats/snapshots/pre
ls pyats/snapshots/pre/

slides.name: write-validation-job
slides.script: Lines one through twenty four create validate_vlans dot py — a minimal aetest job. CommonSetup connects lab-sw1. Test case parses show vlan brief and asserts VLAN one hundred appears in output. CommonCleanup disconnects. This is the post-change validation the exam describes — pyats run job against a testbed. Adjust the VLAN ID to match your intent file from course two.
slides.title: Validation Job Test Case

slides.name: pyats-run-job
slides.script: After your deploy script or ansible-playbook runs, execute post-change validation. Line one sets request_id for log correlation from module two. Line two runs pyats run job — the primary three point four CLI. Exit code zero means all test cases passed. Non-zero means failed or errored — do not close the change. Line four archives job output under logs slash validation dot json if your easypy job writes reports — check the job log path printed in the terminal.
slides.title: Post-Change pyats run job
slides.code:
export REQUEST_ID=chg-5501
export LAB_SSH_PASSWORD='lab-only'
pyats run job pyats/jobs/validate_vlans.py \
  --testbed pyats/testbed.yaml \
  --archive-dir logs/pyats/
echo "exit code: $?"

slides.name: pyats-diff-after-change
slides.script: When validation fails or you need proof of delta, compare snapshots. Line one runs learn again into pyats slash snapshots slash post. Line two runs pyats diff between pre and post directories — output highlights what changed on lab-sw1. Use diff output in RCA from module two — attach request_id chg-5501 in your ticket. The exam may show diff snippets and ask whether the change matched intent.
slides.title: Compare With pyats diff
slides.code:
pyats learn vlan brief --devices lab-sw1 \
  --testbed pyats/testbed.yaml \
  --output pyats/snapshots/post
pyats diff pyats/snapshots/pre pyats/snapshots/post

slides.name: rollback-on-validation-failure
slides.script: When pyats run job exits non-zero, run rollback before manual CLI heroics. Line one logs rollback start with the same request_id. Line two checks out previous intent from Git — course two netops_iac_lab or a tagged release. Line three re-runs ansible-playbook or your deploy script. Line four re-runs pyats run job — must pass before you mark rollback complete. Wire this sequence as a GitLab manual job triggered on post-validation failure.
slides.title: Automated Rollback Sequence
points: Validation fail — stop the change ticket
Revert intent in Git and re-deploy
Re-run pyats run job to confirm
slides.code:
export REQUEST_ID=chg-5501
git -C ../netops_iac_lab checkout main -- intended/vlans.yaml
ansible-playbook ../netops_iac_lab/playbooks/deploy_vlans.yml
pyats run job pyats/jobs/validate_vlans.py --testbed pyats/testbed.yaml

slides.name: troubleshoot-pyats-failures
slides.script: pyATS failures cluster into four exam-friendly buckets. Connection failed — wrong IP, credential, or SSH blocked — fix testbed and firewalls. Parser failed — show command output changed between IOS XE versions — pin image or adjust test case. Assertion failed — network state wrong — real validation catch; investigate deploy not pyATS install. Job error — Python typo in test case — read traceback in easypy log. Always read the first failed test case section name in the report.
slides.title: Troubleshoot pyATS Job Output
points: Connection — testbed IP, creds, SSH
Parser — IOS version vs genie parser
Assertion — intent did not land on device
slides.code:
pyats run job pyats/jobs/validate_vlans.py --testbed pyats/testbed.yaml --no-archive
# read: FAILED >> ValidateVlans.vlan_100_present

Validation proves state; it does not encrypt your SSH passwords or sign your gRPC sessions. Module four covers CA-signed TLS deployment and secure coding — input validation, authentication, secret management — exam objectives three point five and three point six. modules.title: Security Operations
modules.subtitle: TLS, PKI, and Secure Automation Coding

slides.name: validation-to-security-bridge
slides.script: Module three proved VLAN intent landed on the device with pyATS. Module four asks what an attacker sees if they sniff your lab traffic or clone your Git repo. Plaintext SSH passwords in testbed YAML, HTTP webhooks on port eight zero eight zero, and gRPC telemetry without TLS are acceptable only in isolated sandboxes. Production automation needs CA-signed certificates, authenticated APIs, validated inputs, and secrets stored outside code. Exam objectives three point five and three point six live here — describe obtaining and deploying CA-signed TLS certificates, and implement secure coding for input validation, authentication, and secret management. You will harden netops_ops_lab and tie secrets into your GitLab pipeline from course two.
slides.title: Validation Proves State — Security Protects Access

slides.name: plaintext-secrets-risk
slides.script: Two habits from earlier modules must not reach production. First — credentials in Git. pyATS testbed YAML with a literal password, Ansible inventory with admin slash password one two three, API tokens in playbooks. Anyone with repo read access owns your network. Second — cleartext transports. Module one telemetry with grpc-tcp no TLS, module two HTTP webhooks, unencrypted syslog across untrusted links. Attackers replay tokens or inject fake webhook events. Security operations means encrypting sensitive channels and removing secrets from files developers commit daily.
slides.title: What Must Leave the Lab
points: Two habits from earlier modules must not reach production
Git pyATS testbed YAML with a literal password
API tokens in playbooks Anyone with repo read access

slides.name: what-tls-protects-in-automation
slides.script: TLS — Transport Layer Security — encrypts data in flight and proves you reached the intended server. For network automation that covers HTTPS GitLab webhooks, gRPC telemetry with TLS, REST APIs on controllers, and HTTPS downloads of intent files. TLS does not replace authentication — it protects credentials and payloads on the wire. It does not validate network config — that is pyATS from module three. Pair TLS on the transport with token or certificate authentication on the application. Exam three point five focuses on how you obtain and install the server certificate, not every cipher suite name.
slides.title: TLS in Plain Language
points: TLS — Transport Layer Security — encrypts data
For network automation that covers HTTPS GitLab
TLS REST APIs on controllers and HTTPS downloads of

slides.name: pki-plain-language
slides.script: Public Key Infrastructure, PKI, is the trust system behind TLS. A Certificate Authority, CA, signs certificates that bind a public key to a hostname or organization. Your server holds the certificate and private key. Clients trust the CA root or intermediate chain installed in their trust store. In enterprise networks an internal CA — Microsoft AD CS, OpenSSL private CA, or Cisco PKI — issues certs for devices and automation services. Self-signed certs work in lab but clients must manually trust them; CA-signed certs chain to a root your devices already trust — exam three point five expects you to describe that obtain-and-deploy flow.
slides.title: PKI Building Blocks
points: Public Key Infrastructure PKI is the trust system
TLS A Certificate Authority CA signs certificates that bind
Your server

slides.name: obtain-ca-signed-cert-process
slides.script: Exam three point five — describe the process to obtain and deploy CA-signed TLS certificates. Step one — generate a private key on the server or secure enrollment host. Step two — create a Certificate Signing Request, CSR, with subject common name matching the DNS name clients use. Step three — submit CSR to the CA — internal portal or public CA for lab. Step four — receive signed certificate plus intermediate chain files. Step five — install cert and key on the service — nginx, GitLab runner, IOS XE trustpoint for gRPC. Step six — configure clients to trust the CA or full chain. Step seven — verify with openssl s_client or curl minus v before pointing production automation.
slides.title: Obtain and Deploy CA-Signed Certs
points: Exam three point five — describe the process
Certificate Signing Request CSR with subject
DNS name clients use Step three — submit CSR

slides.name: self-signed-vs-ca-signed
slides.script: Self-signed certificates sign themselves — fast for lab, but every client must import your custom trust anchor or disable verification — never in production. CA-signed certificates chain to an enterprise or public root already in the trust store — browsers and IOS trustpoints accept them when hostname matches. Lab module four uses a local CA you create with openssl to mimic enterprise flow without buying a public cert. Production uses your organization's CA process and change control. The exam contrasts when each is appropriate — lab versus production, not which openssl flag to memorize.
slides.title: Self-Signed vs CA-Signed

slides.name: deploy-tls-on-automation-services
slides.script: Deploy means matching certificate to the service clients actually call. Webhook handler — terminate TLS in nginx or Python with cert and key files, GitLab webhook URL becomes https. gRPC telemetry — IOS XE trustpoint references CA cert, telemetry receiver uses server cert on port five seven five zero zero. GitLab runner to device APIs — runner trusts device cert or presents client cert if mutual TLS required. Ansible over HTTPS network APIs — verify equals yes with a custom CA bundle. Document which file is cert, which is key, which is chain — exam questions show a folder of PEM files and ask installation order.
slides.title: Where TLS Lands in Your Stack
points: Deploy means matching certificate to the service clients actually
Webhook handler — terminate TLS
Python with cert

slides.name: secure-coding-three-pillars
slides.script: Exam three point six — implement secure coding practices for input validation, authentication, and secret management. Input validation — never trust YAML, JSON, or API parameters without schema checks — reject VLAN IDs outside policy before ansible runs. Authentication — prove who calls your script or webhook — tokens, mutual TLS, or SSH keys, not shared passwords in code. Secret management — load credentials from environment variables, vaults, or GitLab masked CI variables — never commit them. These three pillars apply to every Python deploy script, pipeline job, and webhook handler you built in courses one through three.
slides.title: Secure Coding — Three Pillars
points: Exam three point six — implement secure coding practices
Input validation — never trust YAML JSON or API
VLAN IDs outside policy before ansible runs

slides.name: input-validation-for-automation
slides.script: Input validation blocks malicious or accidental bad intent early. Parse YAML with safe_load, not load. Validate against JSON Schema or YANG models from course two — VLAN ID range one to four zero nine four, names match regex. Reject path traversal in file arguments — no dot dot slash in user-supplied paths. Sanitize data before shell commands — never os dot system with f-strings from webhook JSON. GitLab prevalidation stage runs yamllint and schema checks — secure coding extends that with explicit Python asserts before deploy. Exam three point six may show a webhook payload and ask what validation is missing.
slides.title: Input Validation
points: Input validation blocks malicious or accidental bad intent early
Parse YAML with safe_load not load Validate against JSON
From course two — VLAN

slides.name: authentication-vs-secret-storage
slides.script: Authentication proves identity — API bearer token in Authorization header, client certificate in mutual TLS, SSH public key on the device. Secret management stores credentials safely — HashiCorp Vault dynamic secrets, Ansible Vault encrypted files, GitLab CI masked variables injected at runtime. Confusing the two fails audits: encrypting a password file without rotation is weak secret management; putting a token in Git is neither auth nor management done right. pyATS testbed should reference os dot environ for passwords; GitLab injects LAB_SSH_PASSWORD as a protected variable.
slides.title: Authentication vs Secret Management
rightItems: Vault, CI variables, env at runtime

slides.name: secret-management-practices
slides.script: Practical secret management for AUTOCOR labs and exams. One — dot gitignore for local env files and keys. Two — GitLab Settings CI slash CD Variables with masked and protected flags for LAB_SSH_PASSWORD and WEBHOOK_SECRET. Three — rotate after any leak — new token, revoke old. Four — least privilege — automation account changes VLANs only, not enable secret. Five — audit access to variable settings. Ansible Vault encrypts files you may commit — password in CI variable only. Module four lab refactors pyats testbed and webhook handler to read secrets from environment, then adds TLS to the webhook listener.

slides.name: create-lab-ca-and-csr
slides.script: Lines one through eight create a lab root CA — not for production, but mirrors enterprise CSR flow for exam three point five. Lines ten through fourteen generate a server private key and CSR with common name localhost — add subjectAltName DNS localhost for modern TLS clients. You submit server dot csr to the CA; in lab you sign it yourself on the next slide. Protect ca-key dot pem — if leaked, attackers mint trusted certs for your lab.
slides.title: Generate CA, Key, and CSR
slides.code:
mkdir -p tls
cd tls
openssl genrsa -out ca-key.pem 4096
openssl req -x509 -new -nodes -key ca-key.pem -sha256 -days 365 \
  -subj "/CN=NetOps Lab CA" -out ca-cert.pem
openssl genrsa -out server-key.pem 2048
openssl req -new -key server-key.pem -out server.csr \
  -subj "/CN=localhost"

slides.name: sign-and-verify-server-cert
slides.script: Line one signs the CSR with your lab CA — output server-cert dot pem. Line two builds a chain file for nginx. Lines four through six verify the certificate against the CA and test TLS with openssl s_client against a listener you start on port eight four four three. Exam three point five asks you to describe this deploy-and-verify sequence; you should recognize each artifact — key, cert, chain, CA root for client trust.
slides.title: Sign CSR and Verify TLS
slides.code:
openssl x509 -req -in server.csr -CA ca-cert.pem -CAkey ca-key.pem -CAcreateserial \
  -out server-cert.pem -days 365 -sha256
cat server-cert.pem ca-cert.pem > server-chain.pem
openssl verify -CAfile ca-cert.pem server-cert.pem
openssl s_client -connect localhost:8443 -CAfile ca-cert.pem </dev/null

slides.name: deploy-https-webhook
slides.script: Lines one through sixteen replace the module two HTTP handler with an SSL-wrapped HTTPServer on port eight four four three using server-cert and server-key. GitLab webhook URL becomes https colon slash slash your-host colon eight four four three slash hook — upload ca-cert to GitLab or use a cert GitLab trusts. Line eighteen curls with minus k only in lab if trust is not configured — production must verify CA. WEBHOOK_SECRET from environment validates caller on line ten — secure coding three point six authentication.
slides.title: HTTPS Webhook With TLS
slides.code:
cat > webhook/handler_tls.py << 'EOF'
import json, os, ssl, datetime
from http.server import HTTPServer, BaseHTTPRequestHandler
SECRET = os.environ.get("WEBHOOK_SECRET", "")
class Hook(BaseHTTPRequestHandler):
    def do_POST(self):
        if SECRET and self.headers.get("X-Gitlab-Token") != SECRET:
            self.send_response(401); self.end_headers(); return
        n = int(self.headers.get("Content-Length", 0))
        body = json.loads(self.rfile.read(n) or b"{}")
        print(json.dumps({"ts": datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat()+"Z",
               "kind": body.get("object_kind")}), flush=True)
        self.send_response(200); self.end_headers()
ctx = ssl.SSLContext(ssl.PROTOCOL_TLS_SERVER)
ctx.load_cert_chain("tls/server-cert.pem", "tls/server-key.pem")
HTTPServer(("0.0.0.0", 8443), Hook).socket = ctx.wrap_socket(
    HTTPServer(("0.0.0.0", 8443), Hook).socket, server_side=True)
EOF
WEBHOOK_SECRET=lab-token python webhook/handler_tls.py

slides.name: refactor-secrets-to-environment
slides.script: Remove literal passwords from pyats testbed — exam three point six secret management. Lines one through four write testbed dot yaml dot example with dollar brace LAB_SSH_PASSWORD placeholder documented. Lines six through eight load dot env locally — file gitignored. Line ten runs pyats with variables exported. GitLab pipeline uses Settings CI slash CD Variables with masked LAB_SSH_PASSWORD instead of dot env on the runner.
slides.title: Externalize pyATS Credentials
slides.code:
cat > pyats/testbed.yaml.example << 'EOF'
# Copy to testbed.yaml locally; set LAB_SSH_PASSWORD in .env
testbed:
  devices:
    lab-sw1:
      credentials:
        default:
          username: admin
          password: "${LAB_SSH_PASSWORD}"
EOF
cat > .env.example << 'EOF'
LAB_SSH_PASSWORD=change-me
WEBHOOK_SECRET=change-me
EOF
echo ".env" >> .gitignore
set -a && source .env && set +a && pyats run job pyats/jobs/validate_vlans.py --testbed pyats/testbed.yaml

slides.name: validate-intent-before-deploy
slides.script: Secure coding input validation — lines one through twenty create validate_intent dot py that loads vlans dot yaml, asserts each VLAN ID is integer between one and four zero nine four, and name matches alphanumeric pattern. Exit code one on violation — wire as GitLab prevalidation job before deploy from course two. Reject webhook-supplied VLAN IDs through this script instead of trusting JSON blindly.
slides.title: Input Validation Script
slides.code:
cat > scripts/validate_intent_secure.py << 'EOF'
import re, sys, yaml
MAX_VLAN = 4094
NAME_RE = re.compile(r"^[A-Za-z0-9_]{1,32}$")
data = yaml.safe_load(open(sys.argv[1]))
for v in data.get("vlans", []):
    vid = v.get("id")
    if not isinstance(vid, int) or not 1 <= vid <= MAX_VLAN:
        sys.exit(f"invalid vlan id: {vid}")
    if not NAME_RE.match(v.get("name", "")):
        sys.exit(f"invalid vlan name: {v.get('name')}")
print("intent valid")
EOF
python scripts/validate_intent_secure.py ../netops_iac_lab/intended/vlans.yaml

slides.name: gitlab-masked-ci-variables
slides.script: Wire secrets into course two pipeline without committing them. In GitLab project Settings CI slash CD Variables add LAB_SSH_PASSWORD masked and protected, WEBHOOK_SECRET masked, and TLS paths if jobs call HTTPS services. Reference in dot gitlab-ci dot yml as dollar LAB_SSH_PASSWORD — GitLab injects at runtime. Never echo secrets in job logs — exam three point six. Rotate variables when anyone leaves the team or a laptop is lost.
slides.title: GitLab Masked Variables
points: Masked + protected CI variables
Reference as $VAR in job script
Never print secrets in job logs

variables:

deploy:dry-run:

slides.name: troubleshoot-tls-handshake
slides.script: TLS failures on exams and in lab map to familiar symptoms. Certificate verify failed — wrong CA file, expired cert, or hostname mismatch — CN localhost but client uses IP. Handshake failure — key does not match cert or wrong file order in nginx. GitLab webhook SSL verification failed — upload CA or use publicly trusted cert. Client still sends HTTP to HTTPS port — update webhook URL scheme. Use openssl s_client and curl minus v for evidence before changing configs randomly.
slides.title: Troubleshoot TLS Handshake
points: Hostname must match CN or SAN
Key pairs with correct cert file
Client trusts CA in chain
slides.code:
openssl x509 -in tls/server-cert.pem -noout -dates -subject
openssl s_client -connect localhost:8443 -servername localhost -CAfile tls/ca-cert.pem

modules.title: Secure Zero-Touch Operations Capstone

slides.name: ops-capstone-intro
slides.script: Welcome to the course three capstone. You have two lab roots — netops_iac_lab from course two with Git, GitLab CI/CD, and VLAN intent, and netops_ops_lab from course three with telemetry, logging, pyATS validation, and TLS-hardened webhooks. The capstone is not a new toolchain. You merge a small intent change on netops_iac_lab, let the pipeline run secure prevalidation, deploy with a request_id logged to netops_ops_lab, confirm telemetry still streams, run pyats run job post-validation, and receive an HTTPS webhook event when the pipeline finishes. Zero-touch means no manual SSH heroics in the change window — the automated chain operates, validates, and secures itself. If modules one through four made sense, you can follow this section.
slides.title: Capstone: Secure Zero-Touch Ops

slides.name: capstone-integrates-four-modules
slides.script: Module four ended with HTTPS webhooks and secrets in CI variables. The capstone runs the full operate-validate-secure story in order. Module one — telemetry stream still healthy in Prometheus during the change. Module two — JSON deploy logs and syslog lines share request_id chg-capstone. Module three — pyats run job must pass after deploy. Module four — webhook uses TLS and WEBHOOK_SECRET; pyATS reads LAB_SSH_PASSWORD from env not Git. You add VLAN three fifty to intended slash vlans dot yaml in netops_iac_lab — same pattern as course two capstone — but the pass criteria include ops gates, not only green GitLab stages.

points: Module four ended with HTTPS webhooks and secrets
Module one — telemetry stream still healthy in Prometheus
JSON deploy logs

slides.name: capstone-two-repo-layout
slides.script: Know which repo owns which concern before the lab. netops_iac_lab — intended slash vlans dot yaml, dot gitlab-ci dot yml, playbooks slash deploy_vlans dot yml from course two. netops_ops_lab — docker-compose for Prometheus and rsyslog, pyats slash jobs slash validate_vlans dot py, webhook slash handler_tls dot py, tls slash PEM files from module four. The pipeline runs in iac repo; ops repo supplies validation, observability, and secure notification. CI jobs call across directories with relative paths or clone both repos on the runner — this capstone uses sibling folders on one runner.

slides.name: zero-touch-vs-manual-ops
slides.script: Manual operations — engineer merges, SSHs to switches, runs show commands, pastes logs in chat, hopes nothing broke. Zero-touch operations — merge triggers pipeline, prevalidation runs validate_intent_secure dot py, deploy emits structured logs, pyATS asserts device state, telemetry dashboards show no silent regression, HTTPS webhook posts result to your handler with authentication. The exam does not use the marketing phrase zero-touch; it tests the components — logging strategy, pyATS validation, TLS, secure secrets — wired in sequence. This capstone is that sequence.
slides.title: Manual Ops vs Zero-Touch

slides.name: seven-steps-ops-runbook
slides.script: Run the capstone in this order. One — start ops stack: Prometheus, rsyslog, HTTPS webhook handler. Two — edit VLAN three fifty in netops_iac_lab on a feature branch. Three — local validate_intent_secure and pipeline prevalidation green. Four — merge to main; deploy job runs with REQUEST_ID chg-capstone. Five — confirm telemetry metrics still flow and grep logs for request_id. Six — pyats run job post-validation from netops_ops_lab. Seven — read HTTPS webhook payload and pipeline post-validation; rollback if pyATS fails. Stop at the first red gate and use module two RCA on the logs.

Validation — pyats run job exit code zero — three point four. Security — HTTPS webhook with token and secrets from CI variables — three point five and three point six. HTTPS webhook handler must print a pipeline event JSON line. If any pillar is missing, the change is not zero-touch complete — return to the module that owns that gap.

Line two verifies handler printed JSON with build_status success. Configure GitLab project webhook URL to https colon slash slash your-runner colon eight four four three slash hook for real pipelines — upload tls slash ca-cert dot pem for SSL verification in GitLab settings.

curl -sk -X POST https://localhost:8443/hook \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -H 'X-Gitlab-Token: lab-token' \
  -d '{"object_kind":"pipeline","build_status":"success","request_id":"chg-capstone"}'

slides.name: capstone-simulate-validation-failure
slides.script: Optional drill — exam three point three and four together. Line one runs deploy with SIMULATE_FAILURE so logs show ERROR. Line two pyats should still run — may fail assertion if device state wrong. Line three demonstrates rollback from module three — revert intent and re-deploy. Line four re-run pyats — must pass after rollback. Treat as RCA practice not required for first pass.
slides.title: Drill: Fail, Rollback, Re-Validate
slides.code:
export REQUEST_ID=chg-capstone-fail
SIMULATE_FAILURE=1 python scripts/run_deploy.py 2>&1 | tee logs/deploy-fail.json
git -C ../netops_iac_lab checkout main -- intended/vlans.yaml
REQUEST_ID=chg-capstone-fail ansible-playbook ../netops_iac_lab/playbooks/deploy_vlans.yml
pyats run job pyats/jobs/validate_vlans.py --testbed pyats/testbed.yaml
